lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market interior-design firms that sell repeat or recurring outsourced planning, design, specification, and project-administration services to external residential, commercial, hospitality, healthcare, or institutional clients.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off decorating-only engagements without a repeat-service relationship; captive in-house design teams; furniture retailers' internal design desks; architectural firms classified elsewhere; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External clients purchase project-based professional services, commonly billed as fixed or staged design fees, hourly consulting, procurement-related fees, or a combination; repeat work comes from developers, operators, architects, and returning homeowners rather than a subscription model.

a.rationale: Interior designers' documented work includes client discovery, preliminary plans, material specification, timelines and cost estimates, orders, and project coordination (S3). AI can reduce drafting, visualization iteration, specifications, research, estimation support, and routine communication, but site observation, spatial judgment, client trust, code-aware decisions, and contractor coordination constrain substitution. The 2023 specialized-design staffing table places interior designers at 18.35 percent of employee headcount and also shows material management, project-management, marketing, and office work (S2), supporting a meaningful but not dominant wage-weighted opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-billing study from a representative sample of interior-design firms, split among concept design, drafting and documentation, sourcing, client service, procurement, and construction administration; paired before-and-after AI workflow hours and payroll data.
caveats: The OEWS source is NAICS 541400, Specialized Design Services, rather than six-digit NAICS 541410 and excludes self-employed workers.
No source directly measures task-level AI substitution for this industry, so the allocation is bounded judgment rather than an observed automation rate.

rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should capture assistive workflows in drafting, visualization, proposals, specifications, and administrative coordination, but the role requires accurate drawings, awareness of building codes and inspection regulations, client communication, site visits, and coordination with architects, engineers, and contractors (S3). Those quality and liability controls limit the share of exposure that can be operationalized.
rho.change_evidence: A structured survey of firm principals and production leaders covering deployed tools, production workflows, measured cycle-time changes, review rates, errors, and the share of deliverables accepted without substantial human rework.
caveats: Implementation will differ sharply between decorating-led residential studios and firms that produce permit or construction documents.
This estimate excludes demand, pricing, and valuation effects.

e.rationale: The code includes planning, designing, and administering interior projects across hospitality, healthcare, institutional, commercial, corporate, and residential work, as well as aesthetic-only decorating consultants (S1). The screen retains only firms with repeat outsourced service relationships. Developer, operator, architect, and returning-client work can qualify, whereas much residential and decorating work is episodic; this mixture supports a minority-to-material eligible share.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level revenue-concentration and client-cohort data from a targeted broker, accounting-firm, or industry-association survey, identifying repeat-client revenue and the service scope of lower-middle-market firms.
caveats: No public source found that reports recurring or repeat revenue specifically for six-digit NAICS 541410 lower-middle-market firms.
Eligibility is a firm characteristic and should not be inferred from establishment counts alone.

s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer likelihood is bounded judgment because no identified public dataset measures qualifying control transfers for the eligible lower-middle-market subset. The occupation has a substantial self-employed component, 21.2 percent in 2024 (S3), which is consistent with owner-led practices but does not establish a sale rate. Transfers require a portable client base and operating system rather than an owner-centered personal brand.
s5.change_evidence: Closed transaction data from specialty business brokers and private-equity databases, plus owner-age and succession-intent survey results, filtered to interior-design services and the defined EBITDA band.
caveats: Self-employment is an occupation-wide statistic, not a measure of ownership or sale propensity for qualifying firms.
The estimate excludes closures without transferable operations and internal reorganizations by definition.

q.rationale: Project-based professional fees give a firm some ability to retain internal productivity gains during an agreed scope, particularly where fixed or staged fees prevail. Retention is reduced at renewal and in competitive bids because clients can compare design fees and may expect faster deliverables; some work is directly tied to ordering, installation oversight, and external vendors rather than labor time. BLS describes services including cost estimates, material orders, installation oversight, and contractor coordination (S3), reinforcing that pass-through varies by engagement structure.
q.change_evidence: Executed engagement letters and invoice histories from representative firms, coded by billing method, change-order practice, renewal timing, and realized price changes after workflow improvements.
caveats: No public, industry-specific evidence was located for fee pass-through or realized pricing after AI adoption.
The estimate concerns retention of an implemented gross benefit and does not include volume loss or implementation difficulty.

d5.bridge: S3 measures US employment for the Interior Designers occupation across all employers, not constant-price demand for NAICS 541410. It projects 87,100 jobs in 2024 to 89,900 in 2034, a 3 percent ten-year increase. Applying the implied mid-decade change to the current service basket gives an approximately stable five-year demand anchor; the error direction is uncertain because 33 percent of the occupation works in specialized design services while the remainder works elsewhere or is self-employed.
d5.rationale: Renovation demand, building-code attention, and accessibility needs are cited by BLS as supports for interior-designer employment (S3). The proxy indicates modest rather than rapid underlying demand growth, while the lower bound allows discretionary renovation and construction-cycle weakness.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted, six-digit industry receipts segmented by client type and a forward pipeline of commercial renovation, hospitality, healthcare, and residential remodel projects from construction-data providers.
caveats: Employment is not a direct measure of constant-price, constant-quality service quantity.
The occupation projection spans employers outside NAICS 541410 and runs over ten years rather than the specified horizon.

o.rationale: A substantial share of future interior-design demand should still require an accountable operator because the documented role includes understanding space use, producing drawings, complying with codes and accessibility requirements, client communication, site visits, and coordination with builders and other professionals (S3). Software can enable self-service visualization and routine layouts, especially in low-complexity residential work, so the estimate does not treat all current demand as durable operator work.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and win-loss data by project complexity, together with adoption and conversion metrics from self-service design platforms and evidence on whether buyers still retain a licensed or accountable designer for implementation.
caveats: The operator-required boundary varies by state licensing rules, project risk, client sophistication, and whether the work is decorating or construction administration.
This is a forward-looking judgment; no identified source directly quantifies future displacement by software.

narrative.executive_view: Interior Design Services contains repeat-service firms, but the industry also contains episodic decorating and owner-personal practices. AI is more likely to compress production and coordination work than to replace accountable design leadership, making the opportunity dependent on disciplined workflow adoption and client portability.
narrative.ai_impact: BLS lists client discovery, preliminary plans, material specification, cost estimates, ordering, installation oversight, and contractor coordination as core duties (S3). Generative and workflow tools can assist with drafts, visualizations, specifications, research, and routine communication, while code awareness, site conditions, aesthetic judgment, and stakeholder alignment retain a human control point.
narrative.value_capture: Professional design fees can allow a firm to hold productivity gains within an agreed scope, but competitive bidding and renewal repricing can return part of the benefit to clients. Procurement and installation-administration work further make capture engagement-specific rather than uniform.
narrative.firm_availability: The relevant target set is limited to firms with repeat external clients and transferable operating practices. BLS reports that self-employed workers account for 21.2 percent of interior designers, a signal of owner-led work but not evidence that a particular firm is saleable or will transfer (S3).
narrative.demand_durability: BLS projects national interior-designer employment to grow 3 percent from 2024 to 2034 and cites renovation, building codes, and accessibility as demand supports (S3). That is a broad occupation proxy, not a direct forecast of six-digit industry demand, and discretionary projects remain cyclically exposed.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The evidence is strongest on occupational duties and broad employment; it is weak on firm-level recurring revenue, transaction rates, contractual pass-through, and realized AI labor savings. Six-digit firms also vary from aesthetic decorating to complex code-sensitive project administration, so diligence must segment revenue and workflow rather than rely on the NAICS label.
narrative.principal_driver: AI can streamline a meaningful share of documentation, visualization, specification, and coordination work while repeat-client firms retain an accountable design role.
narrative.principal_weakness: The industry's project-based, sometimes owner-personal revenue and lack of direct firm-level transfer evidence limit confidence in target availability and benefit retention.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau NAICS 541410 Interior Design Services definition
sources.supports: Defines NAICS 541410 as establishments planning, designing, and administering interior-space projects and lists hospitality, healthcare, institutional, commercial, corporate, residential, and aesthetic consulting activity.

sources.title: BLS May 2023 OEWS, NAICS 541400 Specialized Design Services
sources.supports: Reports 154,890 total employees; interior designers at 28,420 employees and 18.35 percent of employment; management at 10.24 percent; business and financial operations at 10.13 percent; and art and design occupations at 42.55 percent.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Interior Designers
sources.supports: Reports 87,100 jobs in 2024, projected 89,900 in 2034, 3 percent growth, 21.2 percent self-employed, and 33 percent of workers in specialized design services; documents duties, code and accessibility considerations, client-site travel, and contractor coordination.
